topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. I stared at the rejection email, the typeface mocking me with its sterile neatness. Mark had gotten the position. Mark, who always seemed to have the knack for landing the impossible. I clenched my jaw so tightly I tasted copper. "Of course," I muttered under my breath, the sound raspy. I slammed my laptop shut, the sharp crack echoing in the quiet cubicle farm. My hands were shaking slightly as I shoved the rejection letter into my bag, the paper feeling flimsy and insulting in my grip. I wanted to scream at the universe for conspiring against me, for making Mark look so effortlessly superior.

[story 2]

"Seriously? Again?" I repeated the words, the sound thin and brittle. I paced the length of the small breakroom, my steps quick and jerky. Every time I glanced toward the door, I expected to see Mark strolling in, radiating that infuriating calm he possessed. My knuckles were white where I gripped my coffee mug, the ceramic threatening to crack under the pressure. I felt a hot flush creeping up my neck, a tight band around my throat. I picked up my phone, intending to text him a cutting remark, but stopped myself. The sheer audacity of it—the feeling that my own effort, my qualifications, meant absolutely nothing compared to his—made me want to just walk out and shatter something.

[story 3]

The air in the waiting room felt thick and suffocating. I kept re-reading the job description, tracing the words with my eyes as if searching for some hidden flaw, some oversight that would justify this outcome. My breath hitched in shallow gasps. I watched the receptionist smile politely, a gesture that felt like a deliberate slight. Inside, a deep, churning heat rose in my chest, making my palms sweat. I shifted in my chair, the fabric of my trousers suddenly too tight. I wanted to stand up, to make a scene, to point out the glaring unfairness of the situation, but I forced myself to remain still, channeling the intense internal turmoil into a rigid stillness, a silent, simmering eruption just beneath the surface.
